Catalog Description

Special Topics in... Credit 1 to 5. In-depth study of areas of current student interest

and recent advances; normally used for first time offering of new courses.

Prerequisite: Approval of instructor.

Additional Course Details

This course offers an introduction to reinforcement learning (RL), with an emphasis

on Python-based implementations. Beginning with the theory of Markov Decision

Processes (MDPs) we will review and progressing to state-of-the-art RL algorithms.

Topics include the Bellman equation, properties of value functions and optimal

policies, and key algorithmic frameworks such as Q-learning and policy-based

methods (e.g., policy gradient and actor-critic). The course also explores

applications of RL in diverse domains (inventory control, robotics).

Course Prerequisites

Linear Algebra, Probability. More specifically, grade of C or better in MATH

304 or MATH 323; grade of C or better in DAEN 211 (or equivalent); junior or

senior classification. Proficiency in Python.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

to formulate and analyze stochastic dynamic optimization problems and

to implement, test and validate reinforcement algorithms to learn optimal

solutions in stochastic, dynamic environments.

Notes:

Lecture notes will be provided. Complementary reading material from

Reinforcement Learning  by A. Sutton and R. Barto (2018)

Grading Policy

Homework (20\%)

2 Midterm Exams (in class) (50\%)

Final Project (30\%)

All assignments are expected to be completed individually unless otherwise

specified. Any disagreements regarding a grade received on any graded material

must be discussed within one week of the return of the graded material. No grade

will be changed beyond the one week limit. If you request a regrade, the entire

assignment will be regarded (i.e. if we made a mistake in your favor, this will also be

corrected). Grades will be calculated on the basis of total points earned.

The thresholds for letter grades are:

A: 90-100 B: 80-89 C: 70-79 D: 60-69 F: 59 and lower

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Late work is not accepted.

Course Schedule

Week                        Topic

1.  Markov Chains. Ergodicity. Stationary Distribution

2. Markov Decision Process (MDP), Examples: Inventory Control, Linear

Quadratic Control.

3. Finite Horizon Stochastic Dynamic Programming, Principle of Optimality

4. Bellman equation, Value Iteration,

5. Infinite Horizon Dynamic Programming.

6. Policy Iteration. Euler's Equation.

7.  Midterm : February 24

8. Temporal Difference Leaning

9.   Entropy regularized Q-Learning

10. Policy Gradient.

11.  Actor-Critic methods.

12. Advantage Estimation.

13.  Midterm : Late-March (in class)

14. Introduction to Large Language Models (LLMs)

15. Training LLMs with RL. GRPO.

Additional Course Information

N/A

AI Statement

AI tools are allowed. Specific policies will vary depending upon specific assignment.
